region_of_origin: USA (McDonald's global flagship burger, introduced 1968; sold at McDonald's Singapore)
food_subgroup: Double beef patty burger with special sauce, lettuce, cheese, pickles, onion, and sesame bun; 1 burger ~213g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for McDonald's Big Mac; closest parent 'hamburger' (FOODON_03315507) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Big Mac; A0GBY (fast food beef burger) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT code for this commercial fast food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One McDonald's Big Mac burger (Singapore market) weighing approximately 213g, served as a standalone burger without fries or beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Big Mac — McDonald's Singapore Official Nutritional Information
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional data sourced directly from McDonald's Singapore official nutrition page, which is HPB-compliant. Per-burger values (508 kcal, 26g protein, 25g fat, 43g carbohydrate, 880mg sodium per 213g burger) are confirmed from this source. Per-100g values derived by dividing per-burger values by 2.13. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, vitamins) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference for fast food beef burger (USDA SR Legacy item 21110) used as a secondary cross-reference for micronutrient completeness.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the McDonald's Singapore Big Mac. GI ~68 is estimated based on the white sesame burger bun as the primary carbohydrate source (GI ~70 for white bread/burger bun per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the fat and protein content of the beef patties and cheese, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective glycaemic response. GL calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (68 × 43) / 100 = 29.24, rounded to 29.2. This estimation methodology is consistent with Brand-Miller et al. (2003) mixed-meal GI estimation principles.

hypertension.key_factor: 880mg sodium per burger (413mg per 100g) — approximately 44% of the 2,000mg daily sodium limit in a single food item
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit Big Mac consumption to occasional intake (no more than twice monthly), as a single burger provides 44% of the daily sodium limit, leaving minimal allowance for other meals; a full Big Mac meal with medium fries and a medium soft drink delivers approximately 1,145mg sodium (57% of daily limit).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~68 (medium-high), GL ~29.2 per burger — the sesame bun is the primary glycaemic driver; fat and protein content moderates glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat the Big Mac as an occasional food only, as the GL of 29.2 per burger is high for a single item; the 25g fat content (including ~10g saturated fat and ~0.6g trans fat from beef tallow) may contribute to insulin resistance with regular consumption, and the burger should not be paired with sugary beverages or high-GI sides.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~10g per burger, ~4.7g per 100g), trans fat (~0.6g per burger from beef tallow), and high sodium (880mg per burger) are all adverse cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease or at elevated CVD risk should limit Big Mac consumption to rare occasions, as the combination of ~10g saturated fat, ~0.6g trans fat, and 880mg sodium per burger significantly exceeds recommended single-meal limits for saturated fat (7–10g/day total) and sodium, and regular consumption of ultra-processed fast food burgers is associated with increased LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular event risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory saturated fat and trans fat content offset partially by zinc (~4.3mg per burger) with anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume the Big Mac only occasionally, as the saturated fat and trans fat content are pro-inflammatory; however, the beef-derived zinc (~4.3mg per burger) and calcium from cheese may provide modest benefit, particularly for patients on corticosteroids who require calcium support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: McDonald's Big Mac (Singapore market) is a double beef patty burger served in a three-part sesame seed bun with special sauce (a Thousand Island-style condiment), shredded lettuce, one slice of American cheese, dill pickle slices, and diced onion. The Singapore version weighs approximately 213g per burger, slightly smaller than the US version (227g), and is certified halal by MUIS. Per burger: 508 kcal, 26g protein, 25g fat (~10g saturated fat, ~0.6g trans fat from beef tallow), 43g carbohydrate, and 880mg sodium. Per 100g: 238.5 kcal, 12.2g protein, 11.7g fat, 20.2g carbohydrate, 413mg sodium. The GI is estimated at ~68 (medium-high), driven primarily by the white sesame bun, with a glycaemic load of ~29.2 per burger — high for a single food item. The Big Mac is classified as ultra-processed fast food. Clinically, the high sodium content (44% of the 2,000mg daily limit per burger) makes it unsuitable for frequent consumption by hypertensive patients. The saturated fat and trans fat content are adverse cardiovascular risk factors. For Type 2 Diabetes management, the high GL and fat content warrant occasional-only consumption. The beef patties provide meaningful zinc (~4.3mg per burger) and iron (~4.3mg per burger). McDonald's Singapore nutritional data is HPB-compliant. The Big Mac is globally recognised as the reference standard for QSR burger nutritional profiling and is used in The Economist's Big Mac Index for macroeconomic purchasing-power comparisons.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium) are sourced directly from McDonald's Singapore official nutrition page, which is HPB-compliant and represents exact product data for the Singapore market. Per-100g values are mathematically derived from the per-burger values using the confirmed 213g serving weight. Micronutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for fast food beef burger used as a secondary cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on the white sesame bun as primary carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database methodology.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) is not reported in McDonald's Singapore official nutritional data and is not available in the HPB Singapore database for this specific product as of April 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content is not reported in McDonald's Singapore official nutritional data. Trans fat (0.3g per 100g, ~0.6g per burger) is from inherent ruminant trans fats in beef tallow, not industrial partially hydrogenated oils; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fast food beef burgers. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on the white sesame burger bun as the primary carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by fat and protein content; no direct GI measurement for the Singapore Big Mac exists in the published literature. The Singapore Big Mac is slightly smaller than the US version (213g vs 227g US). All core macros (protein_g 12.2, fat_g 11.7, carbohydrate_g 20.2) are non-zero and sourced from McDonald's Singapore official data.

improvement_actions_taken: Restructured input JSON into RESULT template format.
Added mandatory local-language names: Chinese simplified (巨无霸), Chinese traditional (巨無霸), Malay (Big Mac McDonald's), Tamil (பிக் மேக்), Hindi (बिग मैक) to satisfy G6 for Singapore-market product.
Renamed health condition key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Q7 requirement.
Added 'cardiovascular_disease' health context condition (was missing from input) with high concern level.
Expanded aliases_by_language with all mandatory language entries and verified consistency with flat aliases array.
Documented -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) in notes with reason and source consulted.
Expanded natural_language_description to exceed 200-character minimum with clinical and cultural context.
Added gi_source as structured object citing University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed journal.
Added foodex2_gap_note and snomed_ct_gap_note fields to ontology section.
Confirmed all core macros non-zero; no zero-macro justification phrase required.
